main_text: Introduction I n November 1998, the Royal Netherlands Army (RNLA) intro- duced a new medical examination system, the RNLA Basic Medical Requirements (BMEKL), 1 to replace the previous system (PULHEEMS: all diseases and infirmities that lead to unfit declaration are subdivided into seven categories: physical capacity, upper limbs, locomotion, hearing, eyesight, emotional and mental state). The new system is based on the "workload-capability" model and focuses on the job requirements, whereas the old system was focused on diagnosis and assessed recruits on the basis of the detection of diseases and infirmities. From June 1999 to June 2001, we performed a randomized controlled study. We monitored 352 soldiers with fixed term contracts after being randomized, declared fit-for-duty by either of the systems and then trained. The aim was to assess whether there were differences between the two examination systems with regard to their ability to identify suitable recruits. The two examination systems were compared using two outcome measures over a 2-year follow-up period: medical costs and the number of days fit-for-duty. 2 Soldiers approved using the new system showed a statisti-cally significant higher number of days fit-for-duty (648 days vs. 612) and incurred significantly lower medical costs (EUR 396 compared with EUR 746) than soldiers approved using the old system. Adjustment for other personal characteristics had no effect on these differences. For this reason, we concluded that the new examination system was able to select better recruits than the old examination system. In a previous Dutch study, de Kort 3 studied the association between the pre-employment examination and attrition as a result of illness and found that a medical examination may have only a small effect on workers. In addition, he stated that such an examination still could be acceptable if the risks of the work are high. Notwithstanding the outcome of de Kort's study, the risks of work in the RNLA can be very high, and the new BMEKL examination system is merely based on the actual job requirements as mentioned above. Therefore, in theory, the new system should lead to a higher suitability than the old PULHEEMS system; in concrete terms, more fitness for duty and lower medical costs. The remaining question now was, does the new medical examination system really influence the worker's achievements, or might there be other factors involved? Many factors may influence a workers health and achievements without leading to attrition. Their influence may be restricted to a feeling of discomfort, more visits to the doctor, and a longer sick leave, thus influencing both fitness for duty and medical costs without any effect on attrition. Another Dutch study showed that the effects that health risks have on employees are attributed to the way in which the employees experience these health risks. 5 For this reason, information about the opinions of the workers might help to explain the variation in outcomes such as days fit-for-duty and medical costs that we observed in our study. In fact, such information was collected during this study by means of a self-completed questionnaire to be completed every 6 months, to gain insight into the respondents' experiences of the (health) risks. The question we wanted to answer was: to what extent can the performance differences between the two examination systems (BMEKL vs. PULHEEMS) be explained by the personal experiences reported by the soldiers?Materials and MethodsPopulation and Procedure Two study groups were set up. Participants in group 1 were examined using the old PULHEEMS system and those in group 2 using the new BMEKL system. Between September 22nd and October 16th 1998, 863 candidates were examined, with randomization ensured by changing the examination system every week (Fig. 1 ). This eventually led to the formation of two study groups totaling 352 persons (PULHEEMS study group, N ϭ 166; BMEKL study group, N ϭ 186). Figure 1 shows the period for the study as well as the exclusion criteria. One additional exclusion criterion not shown was exclusion of regular personnel with indefinite contracts.Information We collected data from the medical files of the study population and the central database of the RNLA concerning their personal information. Data on medical costs were collected using the database of the RNLA medical service as well as the database of the health insurance provider. The following data were used: files relating to the pre-employment medical assessment, exit interviews with those leaving the service early, assignment details, the absentee registration system, information relating to rejections, transfers on social or medical grounds, internal medical procedures administration, and health insurance claims. Furthermore, we asked all participants to complete the same questionnaire every 6 months during the study (first at baseline, thereafter once every 6 months for 2 years). The questionnaire used for this study was based on the questionnaire commonly used for the periodic occupational health examination in our country, known in Dutch as "PAGO." Our questionnaire was clustered in the same way as the original 5, 6 (Table I ). The original questionnaire consisted of two parts. One part concerned the way in which health was experienced (clusters 3, 4, 5, and 6 in this questionnaire), and the other part concerned the way in which work was experienced (clusters 8-13 in this questionnaire). Furthermore we added a total of 8 clusters to the original questionnaire for various reasons: (a) to verify data obtained elsewhere (clusters 1 and 2); (b) to assess medical costs (cluster 7); and (c) to include the BMEKL questions (clusters 14-18). These last 5 clusters contained 4 questions derived from common job skills 7 (clusters 14-17). The last and 8th new cluster (cluster 18) concerned the translation of specific military assessment points into questions. All questions from cluster 3 onward were dichotomized. The questions in clusters 3 to 7 referred specifically to the situation in the preceding 6 months.Outcome VariablesFitness for Duty For each candidate, the number of calendar days of fitness for duty during the study period was determined by subtracting the total number of days over a period of 2 years (365 ⅐ 2 ϭ 730 days) by the number of days absent because of illness or other reasons (mainly medical restrictions in respect to operational readiness such as injuries). Absence because of illness was the reason in 95% of the cases.Medical Costs The cost of primary medical support was determined by multiplying the number of general practitioner consultations made during the study period by the average Health Care Charges Board tariff (EUR 17.92). Details of other health costs incurred and claimed were provided from the health insurance provider.Statistics We used multiple linear regression analyses to determine the relationships between the outcome measures and other variables. Because there were two outcome measures (days fit-forduty and medical costs), we performed two sets of analyses. In the first set focusing on days fit-for-duty, multiple linear regression analyses were carried out with days fit-for-duty as the dependent variables, and the examination system as the fixed independent variable together with the following variables in succession: age, family situation, gender, demographic background (urban or rural), smoking habits (cigarettes or not), Military Medicine, Vol. 170, January 2005 civilian education, physical trainability, body fat percentage, maximum cycle ergometric performance capacity (in watts per kilogram of body weight), any relevant medical details encountered during the examination, assignment, type of post and being or not being deployed on a (crisis response) mission. These types of linear regression analyses were repeated using the second outcome measure of total medical costs. We calculated the response rate of both, study for each questionnaire groups as well as the mean score of each cluster, as shown in Table I . Before using the data from the questionnaires completed by the study participants, we assessed the reliability of the clusters of questions by calculating Cronbach's ␣ for each cluster. Table II shows that most clusters met the requirement of an ␣ of at least 0.50, with the exception of a few clusters in the first questionnaire and cluster 5 in the second (ear, nose, and throat). Only clusters 12 (clarity, three questions) and 16 (senses/safety, four questions) consistently did not meet this requirement. In four of the clusters (3, 4, 7, and 17), the same single question was removed throughout to achieve an ␣ of at least 0.50. Clusters 8 and 9 were combined for the same reason. To select which clusters from the questionnaire were associated with the outcome measures, we carried out bivariate linear regression analyses, where the relationship between individual clusters and the outcome measure was examined one at a time. Afterward we carried out multiple linear regression analysis using those clusters that had a statistically significant association with the outcome measure based on bivariate analysis. A p value of less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. All analyses were performed using SPSS version 10.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, Illinois).ResultsResults from the Previous Study In the PULHEEMS group, a total of 399 candidates underwent the medical examination, of whom 304 were declared fit-forduty. Of those 304 candidates, 243 eventually entered service on a fixed term contract; on the basis of the exclusion criteria, 166 of these ultimately formed the study group. In the BMEKL group, 464 candidates were examined, 341 of whom were declared fit-for-duty. Of these, 270 recruits entered service on fixed term contracts, and after the application of the exclusion criteria, 186 recruits made up the study group. On the basis of the information gathered (exit interviews, no pursued claims), we assumed that those who dropped out early left because of nonmedical reasons. There were no important differences between the study groups in the characteristics (personal characteristics and examination data) measured at the beginning of the study period. At the end of the study, we found statistically significant differences between the two study groups with regard to assignment to combat or combat support posts, number of days fitfor-duty, and total medical costs.Analysis of Personal Characteristics and Outcome Variables With one exception, there was no statistically significant association between any personal characteristics and the two out-come measures (days fit-for-duty and total medical costs). Only the deployment on a mission had a statistically significant association with the outcome and then only with days fit-for-duty. Civilian education was not of any statistically significant influence (p ϭ 0.06). The p value of the examination system stayed below 0.05 (Table III ).Questionnaires Completed by the Respondents When only the questionnaires that had been filled in completely were included, respondents completed an average of 2.9 of a possible 5 questionnaires. In total, 28 people never responded, 61 responded only once (mainly at the beginning), 60 responded twice, 48 three times, 79 four times, and 76 five times. There was no statistically significant difference between the study groups with regard to response rate; the overall response fell from 70% to 46% over the course of the study period (Table II ). The responses in the questionnaire were then transformed into cluster scores (Table I ). When we examined the association between these cluster scores and the outcome measure of days fit-for-duty using linear regression analysis, we found that the scores for general health (cluster 3), locomotor apparatus (cluster 4), medical consumption (cluster 7), and military assessment points (cluster 18) were often statistically significantly associated (i.e., p Ͻ 0.05) with the days fit-for-duty. Multiple linear  regression analyses with these clusters and the examination system as the independent variables against days fit-for-duty as the dependent variable then showed (Table IV ) that the days fit-for-duty were influenced 3 times in succession by military assessment points (cluster 18), twice by medical consumption (cluster 7) and general health (cluster 3), and once by both clusters regarding the locomotor apparatus (clusters 4 and 14). When we examined the association between these cluster scores and the outcome measure of medical costs using linear regression analysis, we found that the scores for stress (cluster 6), cooperation (cluster 10), locomotor apparatus (cluster 14), physical-chemical (cluster 15), psychological (cluster 17), and military assessment points (cluster 18) were often statistically significantly associated with medical costs. When we used multiple linear regression analyses with these clusters and the examination systems as the independent variables (Table V ), we found that the influence of these clusters was already evident in the first questionnaire. Each of the clusters, stress (cluster 6),  physical-chemical (cluster 15), psychological (cluster 17), and military assessment points (cluster 18), showed a statistically significant association (p Ͻ 0.05) in 2 instances.Completion of Questionnaires and Nonresponse Because there was variation in the number of questionnaires completed by the participants, we examined the relationship between responder and nonresponders. As expected, nonresponse analysis showed that candidates did not respond if they had been rejected, did not sign up, or left in the early stages of military training. As far as age, education, or examination system was concerned, there was no statistically significant difference between the responders and nonresponders; however, we found that more women did not respond. We then performed a multiple linear regression analysis with the examination system and the number of response times as the independent variables and "days fit-for-duty" as the dependent variable. This showed that the number of response times was positively associated with the number of days fit-for-duty: the greater the number of completed questionnaires, the greater the number of days fit-for-duty. Those who never responded (p ϭ 0.01) and those who responded only once (p ϭ 0.02) showed a statistically significant lower number of days fit-for-duty. In this analysis, the p value for examination system was relatively small (p ϭ 0.09). When we carried out the same analysis with medical costs as the dependent variable, we found no statistically significant association between the number of response times and medical costs (p ϭ 0.40). Further assessment of the differences between those who never responded or responded only once and other study participants revealed that the civil education of those who did not respond at all and those who responded only once was statistically significantly lower than for the rest (p ϭ 0.02).Discussion In the personal characteristics registered by the medical examiner, no factors were associated with the number of days fit-for-duty or medical costs. We did, however, find that a number of other factors were associated with the number of days fit-for-duty. With regard to the questionnaires completed by the study participants, we observed that the response frequency was positively associated with number of days fit-for-duty and that frequent responders had a higher level of civil education than nonresponders. Soldiers sent on an operational mission showed a greater number of days fit-for-duty than soldiers who were not sent on such a mission. Also, the number of days fit-for-duty was positively associated with the way in which soldiers experienced their own health (the higher the better), their locomotor apparatus (the higher the better), and the extent to which medical authorities were consulted. Because these three factors specifically related to the situation over the previous 6 months, their influence may be viewed as temporary. Furthermore, the respondent's self-estimate of capability to complete specific military activities was positively associated with the number of days fit-for-duty. Only factors from the questionnaire were associated with the outcome of medical costs. Specifically, three experiences in par-ticular were repeatedly associated with costs: self-assessed ability to perform under extreme climatic circumstances, self-assessed ability to cope with stress and with psychological demands, and self-assessed ability to meet military task-specific requirements. The direct explanation for the relation between response (as measured by number of questionnaires completed) and fitness for duty is attributable to the level of education; the higher the education the greater the fitness for duty. At the same time, there is no relation between response and medical costs. Because no difference whatsoever could be established between the two groups besides education, the most likely indirect explanation for the above-mentioned findings is that the lack of motivation played a part in the background, for instance where a higher absenteeism occurred without leading to extra medical costs. Because nonresponse is the primary characteristics of the one group, this cannot be proven using the questionnaire. The most plausible explanations for finding that operational deployment on a mission was associated with a greater number of days fit-for-duty are the specific circumstances of the mission and the social control during the deployment. For medical consumption it makes no difference whether or not a person is deployed abroad because all medical costs were measured either away or at home, also if repatriation was required for medical reasons. However, it cannot be ruled out either that the disappointment at not being sent on a mission abroad might have led to increased absenteeism as a result of demotivation. The soldier's self-assessed ability regarding certain fields, as gathered from the questionnaire, were also associated with the Fig. 2 . Factors that increase fitness for duty (ϩ) and are measurable by medical assessment, as well as factors that decrease fitness for duty (Ϫ) and are not measurable by medical assessment in this study. number of days fit-for duty, but this association was limited than the association we found with medical costs. Whereas the coefficient for the examination system in the linear regression model became less statistically significant (an increased p value) after information from the questionnaire was included in the model, the information from the questionnaires could only explain a small percentage of the variation in days fit-for-duty observed. In multiple linear regression analyses with medical costs as the dependent variable, the ␤ coefficients of the clusters concerned are also limited. In this study, we were interested in identifying characteristics that were associated with the ability to function as a soldier, and we found various factors (Fig. 2 ). We were also interested in explaining why the new BMEKL examination system was able to select recruits who were more often fit-for-duty and incurred lower medical costs than the old PULHEEMS examination system. Whereas existing injuries are definitely noticed as a result of anamnesis and physical examination, the risk of future injuries and the future amount of medical consumption cannot be predicted. Internationally, excessively intense training is considered to be one of the main causes of injury problems, [9] [10] [11] but the physical selection beforehand should avoid the need for excessively intense training. Previous internal RNLA studies had already shown that there is a relationship between education and drop-out rates; moreover, determining the relevant lower limit is not a medical matter. The decision of whether or not military personnel will be sent on crisis response missions is not determined by the medical examination. Unlike the old PULHEEMS system, determining whether or not a candidate meets the task-specific requirements is a permanent feature of the BMEKL examination system. According to this list, there was one factor in the examination under the BMEKL system that had the most influence on the difference in fitness for duty. This concerned the working method adopted to assess suitability: to evaluate the ability to meet the task-specific requirements. For medical costs the ability to cope with psychological demands also played a role and this is only tested under the BMEKL system as well. The present findings contrast with the prevailing theory regarding the lack of influence of examinations, highly influenced by the findings of de Kort. 3 Two explanations can be given for this. Even de Kort 3 recognizes that the need for an examination could be more vital if not only possible damage to the employee's own health is to be assessed, but also the possible damage to the health of others. This is precisely the question addressed in the military personnel examinations. Besides that, the risks of the work can be high. A soldier is not appointed for simply one single job, but for a wide range of tasks, including operational deployment during crisis response missions. The difference between our findings and the current theory could therefore be based on the fact that this kind of employment concerns extremely difficult and varied work, both physically and mentally. When suitability for less taxing jobs needs to be assessed, the prevailing theory may remain applicable. A second explanation may lie in the fact that the requirements are too tough in comparison to the actual job requirements, meaning that many perfectly suitable candidates are rejected as a result. This explanation seems less likely than the first. There is of course no gold standard, which means that it cannot really be assessed properly; however, there was no statistically significant difference between the two examination systems in the percentage of rejected candidates. It could still be true that both systems are "too strict." In conclusion, several factors are associated with both fitness for duty and medical costs of RNLA soldiers (Fig. 2 ): the level of education, injuries, actual operational deployment and the BMEKL examination system itself. The superior performance of the new BMEKL system seems partly attributable to the assessment of the ability to meet the task-specific requirements. Yet, the primary mechanism is as yet undiscovered.Fig. 1 . 1 Fig. 1. title: Incidence of Acute Injury Related to Fitness Testing of U.S. Army Personnel
abstract: Objective: This study documented the incidence of acute injuries related to the Army Physical Fitness Test (APFT). Methods: A questionnaire was administered to 1,532 soldiers after they completed a biannual APFT. Self-reported injury responses determined the rates of injuries resulting from the push-up, sit-up, and 2-mile run events and were classified into three categories, i.e., all injuries, performance-limiting injuries, and time-loss injuries. Results: A total of 117 soldiers (injury rate, 7.6%) reported sustaining an injury (all injuries), with 11% attributed to the push-up event, 56% to the sit-up event, and 32% to the run event. Forty-six of these injuries reportedly limited performance (injury rate, 3.0%), and 11 soldiers received a duty-limiting profile (time-loss injury rate, 0.7%), which did not differ among events. Injury rates were not significantly associated with the number of sit-ups performed per week or the number of days per week a soldier participated in physical training and were not greater for soldiers who trained specifically for the APFT. History of previous injury was a significant risk factor for injury. Conclusion: The pushup, sit-up, and run events of the APFT do not pose a considerable acute injury risk to active duty soldiers.

main_text: Introduction M ilitary service members perform a wide variety of tasks that require a high level of fitness. To ensure that military members are adequately fit to train for these tasks, each branch of service conducts physical fitness testing on a regular basis. The fitness tests differ for each branch of service and test the cardiovascular fitness and muscular endurance of service members. The objectives of the U.S. Army Physical Fitness Program are to enhance combat readiness by developing and sustaining a high level of physical fitness among soldiers. 1 The Army currently assesses the physical fitness of its soldiers at least biannually with the Army Physical Fitness Test (APFT), a three-event performance test composed of 2-minute timed push-up and sit-up tests and a 2-mile timed run. The events are scored on the basis of the number of repetitions performed, run time, gender, and age, according to a standard table. 2 The minimal passing standard is 180 points, with a minimal score of 60 points per event, to remain in the military. Most units are highly encouraged to achieve scores of Ն240 points, and the maximal score attainable is 300 points (100 points per event). Although the APFT was originally intended to ensure that soldiers possessed a basic level of fitness, the number of total points achieved on the APFT can be used to tier soldiers competitively for promotions, service schools, and other selection processes. Given the emphasis on the use of APFT results for these purposes, many units focus on specificity-based training to improve test event performance, rather than a broad-based exercise program to improve overall fitness. Concerns have been raised that excessive repetition of sit-ups and high-volume running, in attempts to "train to the test," may contribute to the large number of overuse injuries observed among soldiers; however, this has not been studied. The large number of overuse injuries attributed to physical training (PT) is problematic for the Armed Forces. Back and knee disorders, in particular, are among the top five contributors to disability in the Air Force, Army, and Navy. 3 The incidence of overuse injuries was reported to be between 75% and 78% for men and women completing U.S. Army basic combat training, with the majority of injuries occurring in the low back and lower body (83% and 87% for men and women, respectively). 4 Repetitive motion resulting from high-volume training may contribute to musculoskeletal overuse injuries. Studies indicate that performance of the sit-up substantially increases the load to the spine [5] [6] [7] and may contribute to degenerative changes of the spine when performed over prolonged periods. 8 Training for the run event of the APFT has also been related to overuse injuries of the knee. Numerous studies have related high running mileage (Ͼ48 miles/wk) and an increase in volume of exercise (Ͼ10% per week) to a higher incidence of knee injuries. 4, 9 The question remains, however, whether the APFT, in combination with training for the test, significantly adds to overuse injuries in the Army. Complaints of acute back, neck, and knee pain attributed to the sit-up and run events have been anecdotally reported after administration of the APFT. Testing of the Army sit-up, in particular, came under intense scrutiny when, because of increased reports of neck and back pain, the Navy and Marines discontinued the sit-up for the curl-up (CU) (a modified sit-up) and the Air Force excluded the sit-up as a test event altogether. 10 The Army, however, continues to require soldiers to perform the full sit-up (FSU) with hands behind the head. A concerted effort is currently underway to examine the influence of running, sit-up, and other types of PT that might contribute to the large number of overuse injuries among soldiers. Surveillance of acute injuries reported by soldiers immediately after the APFT might shed light on the complaints that have led to the perception of problems with test events. The purpose of this study, therefore, was to determine the incidence of self-reported acute injuries related to the APFT in a population of permanent party, active duty soldiers, with particular focus on the sit-up event. It was our goal to summarize the type, severity, and anatomical location of injuries reportedly attributed to performance of specific APFT events and to identify possible risk factors associated with these injuries.MethodsSubjects The volunteers for this study were active duty, permanent party soldiers stationed at Fort Bragg, North Carolina. They represented a variety of combat, combat support, and combat service support occupational specialties. All volunteers were healthy, active, and pain free. Questionnaires were provided to 1,920 soldiers from 15 company-sized to brigade-sized elements. We received a total of 1,721 surveys, for a response rate of 89.6%; however, 189 surveys were either incomplete or not accompanied by a recent APFT. The final sample size was 1,532 (89% response rate) for men and women who participated in the APFT and completed the survey. Subjects were briefed regarding the purpose of the study and were informed that participation was voluntary.Study Design This prospective cohort study reported the subjective responses of soldiers exposed to the three events of the APFT. A 51-item questionnaire was administered to volunteers 3 to 7 days after administration of the APFT. The questions addressed demographic factors, self-reported APFT test results, injuries related to the APFT, injury history, and methods of training for the APFT. These data were used to compile descriptive statistics and to explore potential risk factors for injury. No medical record reviews or medical examinations were performed.Injury Classification Injuries were classified into three categories according to selfreported responses to questions regarding (1) all injuries (answered "yes" when asked if they were injured during the recent APFT), (2) performance-limiting injuries (answered "yes" when asked if performance of their military duty was limited as a result of this injury), and (3) time-loss injuries (answered "yes" when asked if they had received a duty-limiting "profile" from a health care provider; a profile in the U.S. Army is a duty restriction and/or limitation of PT). In addition, soldiers were asked whether their injury during the APFT was related to a previous injury. Soldiers were also asked to report the APFT event (or events) during which they were injured, the body part (or parts) injured, and the number of profile days received, if any.Potential Risk Factors for Injury The following potential risk factors for injury were reported in the questionnaire: age, military rank, years of service, and APFT score. Previous history of injury was also reported. Soldiers were asked whether they had ever been injured during a previous APFT and, if so, during what event they were injured. They were also asked to report whether they had ever been injured while training for any APFT event and to what activity they attributed their injury.Methods of Training for the APFT Soldiers were asked whether their PT increased as the APFT test approached, at what point before the test their PT in-creased, and how they changed their training. They were asked to report the number and type of sit-ups they performed each week, the number of miles they ran each week, and the number of days per week they performed unit PT.Statistical Analyses SPSS software, version 11.0 (SPSS, Chicago, Illinois), was used to determine descriptive statistics, Pearson's 2 , incidence rates, and relative risk ratios. Sample size estimates were conducted using Epi Info Software (www.cdc.gov) and resulted in a 99.99% confidence level for our sample size. Significance was determined at a probability level of p Ͻ 0.05.ResultsDemographic Factors Table I shows the age, height, and weight for the men and women who completed the APFT survey. The distribution of units participating in the survey and their ranks are presented in Table II . The APFT pass rate for this population was 97.3%.Fitness Level The fitness level of our volunteer population was determined using the 2-mile run time results and a previously published table that converts 2-mile run times to estimates of maximal oxygen consumption (VO 2max ). 11 The mean run time and estimated VO 2max value for each quartile of our population are presented in Table III . Using these estimates and American College of Sports Medicine Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription 12 to predict fitness, we determined that our population was at or above the 75th percentile for men and women 20 to 29 years of age. Therefore, we can conclude that our study population was a fit group.Injuries Figure 1 presents the numbers of soldiers who reported injuries in each of the three injury categories for all three APFT events combined and the respective incidence rates. The reported incidences of injuries resulting in performance decrements or lost duty time among the 1,532 participants were relatively low (3.0% and 0.7%, respectively). Of those who were injured, 97.5% noted their injury within 24 hours after the APFT. Figure 2 presents the distribution of 124 injuries reported by 117 soldiers, stratified according to injury category and APFT event. The sit-up event showed the highest number of all selfreported injuries (56%); however, of the 11 individuals who sought medical care and received a profile, the sit-up event resulted in only five time-loss injuries. The injury rates per 1,000 APFT participations, according to event, are presented in Table IV . The number of injuries according to body site and APFT event is shown in Figure 3 . Figure 3 depicts only those reporting a performance-limiting injury (n ϭ 46, 3% of total surveyed). The sit-up was reported to be associated with the most injuries at the lower back (15 cases), neck (14 cases), and upper back (5 cases), with no injuries at the foot/ankle, leg/knee, and arm/ hand. The 2-mile run reportedly contributed to injuries at the leg/knee (12 cases), foot/ankle (6 cases), and low back (3 cases).Profile Days Time-loss injuries resulted in a total of 271 profile days for 11 soldiers, among whom 15 injuries were reported. The numbers of profile days associated with the push-up, sit-up, and run events were 90, 114, and 154 days, respectively (some profiles were given for injuries attributed to more than one event). Medical profiles for these soldiers restricted activity in unit PT (four soldiers, 96 days); unit PT and military occupational specialty duties (three soldiers, 74 days); unit PT, military occupational specialty duties, and field training (two soldiers, 44 days); and unit PT, military occupational specialty duties, field training, and deployment (two soldiers, 44 days). Four profiles were given for Յ14 days, and 7 were given for 30 to 35 days.Risk Factors for Injury Injury rates were not significantly related to age, gender, or military rank in this cohort. Furthermore, injury rates were not significantly associated with numbers of sit-ups performed per week (mean Ϯ SD, 297.1 Ϯ 231.2 sit-ups) or related to the number of days per week a soldier performed PT (mean Ϯ SD, 3.9 Ϯ 1.7 days). The mean Ϯ SD running miles per week was 17.4 Ϯ 13.7 miles; however, injury was significantly related to running Ͻ10 miles per week. Soldiers running Ͻ10 miles per week were 1.9 times more likely to be injured ( 2 test, p ϭ 0.002). In addition, it was shown that those who trained specifically for the APFT did not injure themselves more than did those who did not train ( 2 test, p ϭ 0.91). Table V lists the APFT scores for all three events according to gender. The total scores and scores for each fitness event were separated into quartiles, and the presence or absence of injury was compared between the lowest quartile and the upper three quartiles. Soldiers who scored in the lowest quartile of the APFT were 2.3 times more likely to report an injury than were those in the upper three quartiles ( 2 test, p Ͻ 0.001). Soldiers who scored in the lowest quartile in the sit-up event were 1.4 times more likely to report any injury than were those in the upper three quartiles ( 2 test, p ϭ 0.04). Similarly, those who scored in the lowest quartile in the push-up event were 1.5 times more likely to report any injury than were those in the upper quartiles ( 2 test, p ϭ 0.003). For the run scores, soldiers in the lowest quartile were 1.6 times more likely to report any injury than were those in the upper quartiles ( 2 test, p ϭ 0.001). Having a previous injury increased the likelihood of a soldier incurring an injury during the APFT. Two hundred soldiers (13.1%) of all participants reported a previous APFT injury. Of those, 28% were injured during the present APFT. These soldiers were 6.1 times more likely to report any injury and 9.4 times more likely to report a time-loss injury. In addition, 306 soldiers (20%) reported a previous training-related injury. They were 3.3 times more likely to report any injury and 8.0 times more likely to report a time-loss injury. When only sit-up-related injuries among all participants were considered, 104 soldiers (6.8%) reported a previous sit-up-related APFT injury. This group was 11.6 times more likely to report any injury and 7.0 times more likely to report a time-loss injury. One hundred soldiers (6.5%) reported a previous sit-up-related training injury, and these soldiers were 7.7 times more likely to report any injury and 10.3 times more likely to report a time-loss injury. Ten of the 11 soldiers reporting time-loss injuries attributed the injury to a previous overuse, parachute jump, or APFT-related injury.Training Methods Most soldiers (56.2%) reported increasing their PT before the APFT. Of these, 71.6% worked harder during training, 58.5% trained more often, and 40.0% trained longer.Discussion This study is the first to document the incidence rate of acute injuries resulting from fitness testing in a military population. Our results indicate that the majority of soldiers who reported an injury related to the sit-up, push-up, or 2-mile run event of the APFT did not seek professional medical treatment and, for those who did, the incidence of receiving an activity-restricting profile was very low. In addition, although we observed that 56% of all injuries were attributed to the sit-up event, only five of those soldiers sought medical care and received a profile, which suggests that the symptoms were not severe. Indeed, in the one study we are aware of that investigated sit-up-related injuries, only 57 injuries during an 18-year period from 1980 to 1998 were deemed severe enough to report to the U.S. Army Safety Center. 13 Finally, the risk of acute injury was greater for soldiers with a history of previous injury during the APFT, or during training for the APFT, and for soldiers who ran Ͻ10 miles per week or were in the lowest quartile on their APFT score. Of the three APFT events, the sit-up event has received the most criticism for a perceived association with acute injury, specifically to the back and neck. 10, 14, 15 The U.S. Army came under scrutiny when, citing anecdotal reports of sit-up-related injuries, the U.S. Navy replaced the FSU with the CU. The FSU, currently performed by the Army, is performed with the hands behind the head, the knees bent to 90°, and the feet anchored. The upper body is raised to a vertical position, where the base of the neck is above the base of the spine (Fig. 4B ). The CU is performed with the arms folded across and touching the chest, the hands placed on either the upper chest or the shoulders, the heels placed 10 inches from the buttocks, and the feet anchored. The participant curls the body up until the elbows touch the thighs (Fig. 4A ). During the CU, the tendency to pull on the neck during the sit-up motion is minimized, and excursion of the trunk and the angle of hip flexion are decreased. Epidemiological studies are not available, however, that have assessed injury incidence by comparing the FSU with the CU. To best investigate subjective complaints that have led to the perception that the sit-up contributes to military injuries, we used three categories of self-reported injury, i.e., all injuries (any symptom perceived by the soldier to be an injury), performancelimiting injuries (individuals who may choose not to go to sick call), and time-loss injuries (individuals who go to sick call and receive a profile). We observed a greater number of "all injuries" related to the sit-up event than to the push-up and run events. We also observed that the sit-up event resulted in more performance-limiting injuries than did the push-up and run events. The greater number of minor complaints, although not severe enough to warrant a visit to a health care provider, may contribute to the perception that the sit-up event presents an injury risk to service members.      Most military studies exclusively report only incidences of time-loss injuries, since these injuries result in a duty-restricting profile which affects both health and military readiness. The low incidence of time-loss injuries for each APFT event in the present study did not allow sufficient power to ascertain a difference between events. Furthermore, the sit-up event was not attributed to a significantly greater number of profile days than were the push-up or run events. Most soldiers indicated that their performance was limited only in unit PT, and 37% of profile days were given to these soldiers. For these reasons, we con-clude that the sit-up event, as performed by the U.S. Army, does not significantly affect health or readiness to a greater degree than the push-up or run events. History of previous injury is a risk factor for injury in a military population, 16, 17 as supported by our results. We observed that individuals with a history of injury during testing or training for the APFT were at significantly greater risk for injury during this APFT for all events. We also observed that individuals with a reported history of injury during testing or training specifically for the sit-up were at greater risk for sit-up-related injury during this study. Of the 11 soldiers who reported a time-loss injury, only one soldier, who was injured during the run, did not relate the injury to a previous overuse, parachute jump, or APFT-related injury. Although we did not perform a medical record review to ascertain the extent of past injuries, our results suggest that many of the injuries reported might have resulted from aggravation of preexisting conditions. A low level of aerobic fitness is another risk factor for injury in a military population. 4, 9, 18 Although the fitness level of our study population was determined to be at or above the 75th percentile for men and women 20 to 29 years of age, those who scored in the lowest quartile on the APFT were 2.3 times more likely to report injuries than were those in the upper quartiles. In particular, those in the lowest quartile of the run, a measure of aerobic fitness, were 1.6 times more likely to report an injury. We also found that 250 of 1,532 soldiers who ran Ͻ10 miles per week (23% of whom did not run) were at 1.8 times greater risk for injury, which suggests that individuals who are less physically active are at greater risk for injury. Several studies have supported a link between illness and injury among soldiers with lower aerobic capacity. 4, 19, 20 Knapik et al. 4 recently reported that lack of aerobic capacity was associated with a greater likelihood of injury for both men and women during basic combat training, with the least-fit trainees being two to three times more likely to be injured. Our observations support the results of these studies and suggest that a high level of physical fitness could be protective against PT injuries. The use of the FSU by the U.S. Army has been criticized for its lack of specificity to exclusively measure abdominal strength. The U.S. Air Force discontinued the use of the FSU, citing that "the sit-up was not an ideal candidate for us because it doesn't focus solely on the abdominal musculature." 10 An additional criticism is that it is not known whether sit-up test results relate to the ability to perform militarily relevant tasks. The sit-up test is, however, a reliable measure of one aspect of trunk strength that is easy to administer and does not require equipment. Poor performance of the sit-up during the APFT has been linked to an increased risk of injury, indicating that the sit-up can be used as a predictor of fitness. Reynolds et al. 21 studied the physical fitness of infantry soldiers and found that those in the lowest quartile on the sit-up test had two times the risk of lower extremity and low back training injuries than did those in the second highest quartile. Similarly, Knapik et al. 18 studied a cohort of infantry soldiers and found that those in the lowest quartile for sit-up performance were 1.9 times more likely to sustain an injury in the field than were those in the highest quartile. Based on our results, a better case might be made for removal of the sit-up as an APFT test event for individuals with previous injuries.  We observed that injuries to the lower extremity resulting from the 2-mile run event were more prevalent among individuals with a previous training or test-related injury. In Army basic training, 80% of the lower extremity injuries sustained by the least fit are caused by volume overload in training. 4, 19 It has been suggested that there is a threshold at which running more miles during training increases injury incidence without improving aerobic capacity (VO 2max ). 22 In our cohort of volunteers, more than one-half reported increasing their training before the APFT, and most did so within the previous 2 months. Ninetyfour percent trained for the 2-mile run by running long distances, whereas only 65% conducted interval training. For lessfit or previously injured soldiers, interval training might be a more effective way to build speed and stamina than distance running and would allow soldiers to meet the APFT goals without running over their volume threshold. 23 Although we conclude that there is a very low incidence of injury upon administration of the 2-mile run test, certain individuals may be predisposed to overuse injuries if training for the run involves high running mileage. A limitation of our study is that we can report only the incidence of acute injuries, which does not address the possibility that performing sit-ups or other test events repetitively results in accumulated damage over time, particularly among susceptible individuals. We did not observe that age, rank, or time in service was significantly related to acute APFT injury, factors that would support the idea that repetitive performance of pushup, sit-up, or run activities over a period of years predisposes an individual to injury. We did observe that Ͼ90% of our volunteers trained for the sit-up event by performing sit-ups repetitively and performed an average of 297 sit-ups per week. The published literature does support the idea that repetitive flexion and extension of the spine during the sit-up might place a significant amount of strain on the spine, which could theoretically contribute to injury among individuals who are predisposed to degenerative changes of the spine or have weak abdominal musculature. 24, 25 During performance of the sit-up, the abdominal muscles (rectus abdominis, oblique muscles, and transversus abdominis) are recruited to accomplish the CU phase and also act to stabilize the lumbar spine. As the trunk flexes past 30°, the hip flexors (rectus femoris and iliopsoas) contribute more significantly to the muscular activity. 7, 26, 27 When the motion is performed repetitively, as during the APFT, the abdominal muscles begin to fatigue and the hip flexors become increasingly engaged. 14, 28 This increased activity of the hip flexor muscles may increase the risk of acute injury to the back as the trunk hyperextends and the hip flexors pull the lumbar spine into lordosis, resulting in anterior shear forces and compression of the intervertebral discs. [29] [30] [31] The psoas muscle, a primary hip flexor, can act to stabilize the lumbar spine, but it does so at the expense of creating large anterior shear forces at L5-S1. 32 This may occur regardless of the method used (FSU or CU). The strain to the spine, particularly among susceptible individuals, may result in an episode of acute low back pain. It has been suggested that the sit-up test might be performed more safely with controlled speed at a selected cadence, to prevent acceleration of the spine by the hip flexors and to allow greater abdominal force to be developed throughout the range of motion. 33, 34 In conclusion, the present study indicates that the incidence of time-loss injuries, which is a true indicator of how an injury might affect soldier health and readiness, is low after administration of the APFT and is not significantly greater for the sit-up than for the push-up and 2-mile run events. Soldiers with a previous injury or lower fitness level might be at elevated risk for injury during the APFT. Although the results of this study indicate that these injuries do not contribute significantly to soldier health or military readiness, it might be helpful for the command to identify at-risk soldiers. This could be accomplished through fitness testing or injury history questionnaires, to more properly execute a training program that does not induce injury among susceptible individuals.Fig. 1 . 1 Fig. 1. title: A New Pre-employment Functional Capacity Evaluation Predicts Longer-Term Risk of Musculoskeletal Injury in Healthy Workers
abstract: Prospective cohort study. Objective. To determine if a job-specifi c pre-employment functional assessment (PEFA) predicts musculoskeletal injury risk in healthy mineworkers. Summary of Background Data. Traditional methods of pre-employment screening, including radiography and medical screenings, are not valid predictors of occupational musculoskeletal injury risk. Short-form job-specifi c functional capacity evaluations are increasing in popularity, despite limited evidence of their ability to predict injury risk in healthy workers. Methods. Participants were recruited from an Australian coal mine between 2002 and 2009 as part of the hiring process. At baseline, participants were screened with the JobFit System PEFA, and classifi ed as PEFA 1 if they met job demands and PEFA > 1, if not. main_text: M usculoskeletal injuries in the workplace continue to be a signifi cant economic and social problem in industrial nations worldwide. 1 -4 For the Australian mining industry, for example, musculoskeletal injuries account for the largest proportion of workplace injury claims (44%), with body stressing (manual handling) being the most common mechanism of injury (42%) and the back being the most common body part involved (19%). 5 Pre-employment screening that endeavors to identify individuals who are at greater risk of sustaining an injury is one method used in an attempt to reduce workplace injuries. Traditional methods of screening, such as back radiography and medical screenings including strength, fl exibility, endurance, and body composition testing have not been shown to predict subsequent injury risk. 6 -9 Many researchers in the fi eld of workplace injury prevention have highlighted the need for job-specifi c assessments. 10 -13 It is also a requirement of antidiscrimination legislation in many jurisdictions that work-related assessments test against the inherent requirements of the job. 14 -19 injuries were reported by 121 workers, including 35 back injuries from manual handling. Signifi cant differences between PEFA groups were found in time to fi rst injury for all injury types during the long term (any injury: adjusted hazard ratio [HR] = 2.3, 95% confi dence interval [CI] = 1.4-3.9; manual handling injury: HR = 3.3, CI = 1.6-7.2; any back injury: HR = 3.3, CI = 1.6-6.6; back injuries from manual handling HR = 5.8, CI = 2.0-16.7), but not during the short term. An area under the receiver operator curve value of 0.73 (CI = 0.61-0.86) demonstrated acceptable predictive ability for back injuries from manual handling during the long term. Conclusion. JobFit System PEFAs predict musculoskeletal injury risk in healthy mineworkers after 1.3 years of employment. Future research should assess whether use of these assessments as part of a holistic risk management program can decrease workplace musculoskeletal injuries. Key words: musculoskeletal diseases , functional capacity evaluation , occupational injuries , physical fi tness , back injuries , pre-employment screening , manual handling , risk management . Functional capacity evaluation (FCE) testing has traditionally been used in rehabilitation and medicolegal assessments, but generally not in the pre-employment phase because of time and cost restraints. A short-form FCE, comprising only a core selection of functional activities, is more practical with the development of injury or job-specifi c FCEs now recently adopted in the rehabilitation arena. 20 -22 Although more validity research is needed, recent evidence for the predictability of return to work of injured workers using short-form FCEs is promising. [23] [24] However, very limited published evidence is available to justify their use as a predictor of injury in healthy workers. Even with traditional long-form FCE methods, a recent Cochrane Review 25 concluded that there is minimal quality evidence for functional testing in pre-employment screening, despite their increasing use for this purpose. The aim of this research is to evaluate the validity of a job-specifi c pre-employment functional assessment in terms of its ability to predict musculoskeletal injury risk in healthy mine workers in the Australian coal mining industry.MATERIALS AND METHODSDesign This prospective cohort study investigates the relationship between participants' performance in a job-specifi c JobFit System PEFA and their subsequent workplace musculoskeletal injury history. Participant PEFA performance and demographic information were collected at the time of the assessment. Employment data and injury statistics were collected during and at the conclusion of the study period. This project was cleared in accordance with the ethical review guidelines at The University of Queensland by the School of Human Movement Studies Ethics Committee.Participants A coal mine with underground and open cut operations, employing more than 1000 workers, participated in the study from 2002 to 2009. As part of the hiring process, all prospective employees were required to participate in a job-specifi c PEFA. Prospective employees from all operational areas and all occupation types were eligible to participate. Exclusion criteria included: • Current injury or injury requiring medical treatment, time off work or restricted duties in the previous 6 weeks. • Current Worker's Compensation Medical Certifi cate. • Blood pressure higher than 145 mmHg (systolic) or 95 mmHg (diastolic). • Current or past history of a cardiac condition. • Surgery, fractures, or dislocations within the previous 6 months. All participants (n = 1019) signed an informed consent outlining assessment components, risk, and expectations of submaximal physical testing and the precautions that would be taken, the purpose of the assessment, the use and disclosure of the collected information, and the opportunity to discontinue testing at any time. Female employees (n = 95) were excluded, as were participants who were not successful in their job application. The reason for unsuccessful employment was not disclosed to the researcher by the employer, and it is not known what infl uence the PEFA score had on this decision. Male participants who completed the assessment, were hired and employed in the job related to their PEFA assessment were included, resulting in data from 600 participants for analysis ( Figure 1 ).Outcome Measures and Data CollectionPre-employment Functional Assessment The JobFit System PEFA was used to conduct the job-specifi c functional assessments. The test components and criteria for the PEFAs were specifi c for the job for which the participant was applying. The functional demands of the tasks had been previously assessed by a physiotherapist using observation and interview techniques and were broken down into 42 postural tolerances measures and 21 manual handling measures. Task demands were collated using the JobFit System software to produce job demands. Postural job demands that were used for the PEFA criteria in this study included: reaching forward, reaching above shoulder, stooping, squatting, and stair climbing. These were selected on the basis of the job requirements and injury history at the workplace. Manual handling job demands included fl oor, bench, shoulder and above shoulder lifts, and bilateral carry. The maximum weight requirement for each manual handling measure for each job was used as the assessment criteria. Each PEFA contained the following test components and was delivered in the same sequence: musculoskeletal screen, aerobic fi tness test, postural and dynamic tolerances (job specifi c), and manual handling tasks (job specifi c). The jobspecifi c PEFAs could have any combination, and any number, of the postural tolerances and manual handling listed earlier, however, components were always delivered in the same sequence. A functional lifting approach was used. In general, the PEFA is completed within a 1-hour timeframe. Testing procedures were fully explained to the participant prior to commencement and were consistent with those outlined in the JobFit System Training Program. 26 PEFAs were conducted by a physiotherapist, occupational therapist, or exercise physiologist. Performance on the postural and dynamic tolerances tasks and the manual handling tasks were compared with the job demands to determine the JobFit System PEFA score. The reliability of the JobFit System PEFA has been evaluated and reported previously, 27 and was considered suitable with excellent intrarater reliability (intraclass coeffi cient [ICC], 0.94; CI, 0.90-0.96) and good inter-rater reliability (ICC, 0.84; CI, 0.75-0.90).JobFit System PEFA Score The JobFit System PEFA score (range, 1-4) is the overall score of the worker's performance in comparison with the physical demands of the job for which they are applying: • Score 1: Has demonstrated the functional capacity to perform the proposed position as described with no restrictions. • Score 2: Has demonstrated the functional capacity to perform the proposed position as described with minimal restrictions. • Score 3: Has demonstrated the functional capacity to perform the proposed position as described with moderate restrictions. • Score 4: Has not demonstrated the functional capacity to meet the inherent requirements of the proposed position as described. Prior to statistical analysis, raw PEFA data collection sheets from workers who had been injured at work since the time of their PEFA, were reviewed and scored by a blinded independent third party. Of the 121 records reviewed, disagreement was found between the original scoring and the reviewer's scoring for 15 participants. Agreement on the scoring was reached through discussion between the researcher and the auditor.Sociodemographic Records Sociodemographic characteristics including age, job, and department were collected. Employment records including start and fi nish dates and the job in which the applicant was employed were provided by the company's human resources department.Injury Data Injury reports for study participants were retrieved from the company's accident and incident database. The injury database used in the study is the central repository for all accidents and incidents that occur at the company. Employees are legally obligated to report all incidents to their supervisor for entry into the database. The company maintains clear defi nitions for the coding of the injury data. This database captures injuries that may not progress to a workers' compensation claim and was therefore more complete than Workers' Compensation data. Injury severity was in the range from no treatment required through to lost time from work. All sever-ity classifi cations were considered an injury. The aim of the functional assessment was to identify musculoskeletal injury risks arising from overexertion and consequently the analysis was restricted to injuries coded as "sprain/strain." Each narrative record was individually reviewed and coded by the researcher in 3 categories: (1) body part, (2) mechanism of injury, and (3) severity of injury. Injury codes were reviewed by a blinded independent third party for all injured participant records (n = 196). Differences occurred for 31 records before agreement was reached by discussion between the researcher and auditor. Time from commencement of employment to date of injury was also recorded. In the event that a participant recorded more than one injury during the study period, data from all injuries was captured, and data regarding the fi rst injury of its type was included in the analysis. Neither prior medical history nor workers' compensation data at the time of the PEFA, nor that reported by the participant during the medical clearance process were permitted to be captured.Data Analysis Relative risk was calculated for PEFA score and each injury type. The relationship between PEFA score and time to fi rst injury was studied using Cox proportional hazards regression. PEFA scores were dichotomized to PEFA 1 (met job demands) and PEFA > 1 (did not meet job demands) groups for the analysis. The models were analyzed unadjusted and with adjustment for confounders. Potential confounders ( i.e ., age, job, and department) were added to the model one-byone, and if it changed the regression coeffi cient by more than 10%, the variable was included in the adjusted model. Using this selection method, only department was included as a confounder. The Cox proportional hazards model assumes that the hazard ratio (HR) is constant over time. This assumption was checked and the log-minus-log curves and models with time-dependent covariates showed an interaction with time consistently for all outcomes. Therefore, further post hoc analyses were stratifi ed for time with the cutoff for time set at 1.3 years based on the log-minus-log curves. HRs were presented for 0 to 1.3 years (shorter term) and 1.3 to 6 years (longer term) separately. To estimate the predictive ability of the PEFA to discriminate between participants with and without injury, the area under the receiver operator curve (AUC) was calculated with 95% confi dence intervals. An AUC of 1 indicates perfect discriminative ability, whereas an AUC of 0.50 indicates that the discriminative ability is equal to chance. All analyses were done using IBM SPSS Statistics version 20 for Windows. P values were based on 2-sided tests and were considered statistically signifi cant at P < 0.05.RESULTSParticipant Characteristics Of the 600 participants, 427 met the job demands and scored PEFA 1 (71%) ( Table 1 Department explanatory notes: • CHPP is a multistory facility accessed via multiple stairs that washes and prepares coal for transport; workers are predominantly engaged in operating, maintaining, and repairing infrastructure and conveyor belts and may also spend some time in the control room or operating heavy mobile plant such as dozers. • Open cut is the surface mining operations; workers are predominantly engaged in operating heavy mobile plant equipment including rear dump trucks, dozers, graders, water trucks, light vehicles; a smaller number operate electric shovels and draglines. • Professional is the offi ce work environment; workers are predominantly engaged in administrative type activities, but may also participate in fi eld inspections and supervision in the other departments, including accessing and egressing equipment and operating light vehicles. • Underground is the underground mining operations; workers are predominantly engaged in physical labor and operating, servicing, and repairing machinery in standing, walking, and seated positions, which are often awkward with frequent manual handling; ground and light conditions are poor. • Workshop is a surface maintenance and repair facility for heavy mobile plant; workers may also be required to perform activities in the fi eld on uneven ground with seasonal temperature variations; working postures are often awkward with frequent manual handling. ASCO code explanatory notes: • Laborers are generally unskilled blue-collar workers. participants with scores 2 and 3 were collapsed into one group: PEFA > 1 (n = 173) for subsequent analysis. Of the 295 workers who were not employed, 69.2% scored PEFA 1 (n = 204). The median age at time of PEFA was 37.0 (IQR, 29.0-45.0; range, 17.0-62.6) years. There was no signifi cant association between age at time of PEFA and PEFA score ( P = 0.08). The mean duration of employment during the study period was 2.7 years (SD, 2.1). The PEFA groups differed in department of employment ( P ≤ 0.001) and occupation type ( P ≤ 0.001) ( Table 1 ).Injury Rates During the study period from December 2002 to December 2009, a total of 196 sprain/strain injuries were reported by 121 workers. Injury rates per person year for each body location and mechanism of injury are reported in Table 2 . The highest injury rate by body location was injuries to the back/trunk (43 per 1000 person years). Manual handling had the highest injury rate by mechanism of injury at 46 per 1000 person years. Back and trunk injuries associated with manual handling were the largest subgroup (22 per 1000 person years).PEFA Score and Injury Risk A signifi cant increase in relative risk exists for workers who score PEFA > 1 for any injury type with the greatest relative risk being for any back injury from manual handling (RR, 3.0; 95% CI, 1.4-6.1) ( Table 3 ). Statistically signifi cant differences were found between PEFA groups in time to fi rst injury during the longer term, but not the short term, for all injury types ( Table 3 ). These relationships remained signifi cant after adjustment for confounders. The interaction between PEFA and department was not significant with results presented for the total group only. During the long term, risk of injury was 2.3 times greater in the PEFA > 1 group than the PEFA 1 group (CI, 1.4-3.9) but was not significant in the shorter term. Signifi cant group differences were also found for injuries resulting from manual handling (HR, 3.3; CI 1.6-7.2) and for back injuries (HR, 3.3; CI, 1.6-6.6). The greatest difference in injury risk was observed for back injuries resulting from manual handling during the longer term, with the likelihood of sustaining an injury being 5.8 times greater in the PEFA > 1 group than in the PEFA 1 group (CI, 2.0-16.7) ( Figure 2 ). Although acknowledging that the study was underpowered to analyze the predictive value of the individual PEFA scores, an explorative analyses was done, which showed that the HRs were higher for PEFA 2 (HR = 8.8; CI, 2.9-26.3) than PEFA 3 (HR = 3.6; CI, 0.9-15.1), suggesting no linear dose-response relationship. However, confi dence intervals were wide and results should be interpreted with care. The AUC as a measure of the predictive ability of the PEFA for each injury type in the short and long term is presented in Table 3 . Moderate levels were demonstrated during the longer  term, with an acceptable predictive ability of the PEFA for back injuries from manual handling confi rmed with an AUC value of 0.73 (CI, 0.61-0.86).DISCUSSION Performance in a job-specifi c PEFA predicted risk of any injury, any back injury, any manual handling injury, and any back injury from manual handling in a group of 600 Australian coal mine workers during the longer term, but not in the short term. The association between the JobFit System PEFA and injury risk was strongest for the risk of back injuries associated with manual handling. This is the fi rst study to demonstrate the validity of job-specifi c PEFAs in healthy coal miners, and also the fi rst to identify a change in musculoskeletal injury risk profi le over time and for different injury types. The research has a number of limitations, including restricted access to information such as previous injury history, chronic diseases, and educational background that have previously been reported as confounding factors in musculoskeletal injuries. 6 , 23 , 28 The assumption of the accuracy of the company's injury records may be a study limitation; however, there is no reason to predict any reporting bias as a function of PEFA score. The potential positive infl uence of concurrent risk management strategies employed at the workplace ( e.g ., equipment and task redesign, use of personal protective equipment, and risk management training), as well as potential negative infl uencers ( e.g ., productivity demands, variable mining conditions, and shift work including 12-hour shifts), could also not be controlled, however both participant groups were exposed to the same infl uencers. Further research at additional workplaces and in different industries is required to demonstrate the generalizability of the fi ndings. A notable difference between this and other studies investigating the validity of pre-employment functional testing was the identifi cation of a change in injury risk profi le over time. The longer duration of this study and mean employment time of the participants in comparison with others 13 , 29 -31 enabled this trend to be investigated. This study identifi ed what could be described as an initial "honeymoon period" after which time the participant's risk of fi rst injury increased dramatically. Further research is needed to attempt to determine the reason why this increased risk occurs; however, possible hypotheses include increased rate of musculoskeletal deterioration as a result of working at maximum capacity for an extended period; decreased worker and/or employer awareness of, or compliance with, restrictions advised at the time of testing or commencement of employment; further deconditioning from inactivity or other factors; or, behavioral change of the worker toward more risk-taking activities on the assumption that "they had 'survived' thus far and therefore the predictions were wrong." Until such evidence is identifi ed, health professionals, workers, and employers can maximize the opportunity of the "honeymoon period" as a time for physical conditioning, behavioral safety programs, and workplace modifi cations in an attempt to reduce the worker's risk of injury proactively rather than the negative option of not hiring. A second distinguishing feature of this study was the measurement and identifi cation of variable risk profi les for different injury types. Although the PEFA was predictive of 4 different categories of musculoskeletal injuries, it was most predictive of manual handling injuries, and possibly had the strongest prediction for back injuries associated with manual handling because of the number of included activities that tested back function. However, numbers of this last type of injury were low and validation of the current results in a different sample is needed to confi rm these fi ndings. Further research into the predictive ability of components of the PEFA, rather than just the overall score, on different injury types and body locations in healthy workers may give insight into which items are the most predictive and how the PEFA may be shortened or improved. As discussed by Serra et al , 32 the implementation of pre-employment testing programs is a balancing act between protecting a worker and their colleagues from harm, and against protecting them from discrimination. Assessments that are jobspecifi c, objective, and validated play a large role in maintaining that balance, especially when they are part of a holistic risk management program including ergonomic redesign, behavioral safety, and individual health improvement programs.CONCLUSION These results indicate that the JobFit System PEFA may be a valid predictor of time to fi rst injury risk in healthy workers and could be used by employers as part of a holistic injury prevention program. More research is needed to determine the mitigating factors during, or the attenuating factors after, the initial honeymoon period during which the workers' injury risk was lowest. Likewise additional research is needed to identify which components of the JobFit System PEFA are more predictive of different injury types and body locations than others.➢ Key Points There is limited evidence regarding the validity of functional testing in healthy workers as a predictor of workplace injury. This study indicates that the job-specifi c JobFit System PEFA is a long-term predictor of workplace musculoskeletal injury risk. The association between the JobFit System PEFA and injury risk was strongest for the risk of back injuries associated with manual handling during the longer term.Figure 1 . 1 Figure 1. title: Can pre-placement health assessments predict subsequent sickness absence?
abstract: Background Sickness absence is a growing economic problem, due largely to the financial losses it incurs. The ability to identify employees likely to take greater than average sickness absence may provide managers with useful information at the pre-placement stage. Aim To confirm whether specific risk factors identified at the pre-placement health assessment could predict subsequent sickness absence. Methods A total of 400 National Health Service pre-placement health questionnaires were analysed to allocate employees to low-, medium-or high-risk categories for subsequent sickness absence, using the risk table developed by C. J. M. Poole (Can sickness absence be predicted at the pre-placement health assessment? Occup Med (Lond) 1999; 49:337-339) [1]. Subsequent sickness absence was analysed to assess if there was an association between the allocated category and sickness absence taken. Results Mean sickness absence hours per 1000 h worked were 22.5 (95% CI 18.2-27.2) in the low-risk group, 33.6 (27.2-40.7) in the medium-risk group and 44.7 (25.1-69.9) in the high-risk group (analysis of variance, P # 0.002), demonstrating a statistically significant difference in sickness absence taken in subsequent years. Conclusions The results confirmed Poole's hypothesis that future sickness absence can be predicted at the preplacement health assessment. Certain risk factors, namely female sex, age, smoking, history of at least two previous episodes of low-back pain and previous days sickness absence identified at preplacement assessment, predict a greater than average subsequent sickness absence. However, the best model using identified risk factors only predicted 10-12% of the variation in sickness absence.
